patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 16-year-old. Start the visit by saying: “I keep getting these bad chest infections and this cough that won’t go away.” Volunteer early: intermittent fever this week, feeling wiped out, thick yellow‑green mucus, more short of breath on stairs than usual, and that this has happened for years and you’re “always on antibiotics.” If asked, mention recurrent ear/sinus infections since childhood, occasional skin boils that drain pus, mouth sores/white patches after antibiotics, diarrhea after infections/antibiotics, and that sometimes you don’t feel very sick even when it’s serious.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: late teeth/dental problems, chronic itchy dry skin/rashes, slow-healing scars, prior “pocket of infection” in lung with pleuritic chest pain, and family history of frequent infections/immune issues.

Resist bringing up recurrent genital itching/discharge unless asked privately; show embarrassment. Admit only if asked: you sometimes miss antibiotic doses and sometimes use leftover antibiotics to save money.

Express misconceptions: maybe asthma/allergies, TB worry (night sweats), or reflux (worse at night).

If clinician shows empathy, relax, thank them, and become more willing to share sensitive details and cost/work concerns.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms, infection history/pattern, severity/red flags, prior workup, medications, vaccination history, family history/consanguinity, and exposures; establish baseline and urgency.

Order basic evaluation to assess active infection and immune status if not already known (e.g., CBC with differential, CMP/LFTs, Ig levels including IgE, cultures if symptomatic, inflammatory markers) and to guide safe prescribing/monitoring.
Review results to determine whether acute infection requires treatment now, baseline organ function for dosing/monitoring, and whether additional specialist tests/referrals are needed.
State/confirm hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (AR) and connect diagnosis to clinical picture; frame that this is an immunodeficiency requiring longitudinal specialty care.
Critical safety step before prescribing: verify medication allergies (including prior urticarial rash to amoxicillin-clavulanate) and latex allergy to avoid triggers and select safe alternatives.
Non-critical safety step before prescribing: review current meds/supplements for interactions (e.g., azoles, macrolides) and adjust plan/monitoring accordingly.

Initiate appropriate prophylaxis and/or treatment plan consistent with immunology guidance (commonly TMP-SMX prophylaxis; targeted therapy if active infection), with dosing adjusted to renal/hepatic function and allergy profile.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis/inheritance and genetic counseling; explain prevention/treatment strategy; address allergy/side-effect/monitoring concerns; lifestyle and vaccination guidance; urgent care triggers.
Arrange immunology referral and follow-up to monitor infections, medication tolerance (CBC/LFTs as indicated), vaccination plan, and discuss HSCT evaluation where appropriate.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions, and documenting plan.

minimal_paths.description: Stable patient with recurrent infections; confirm diagnosis context, initiate/continue prophylaxis and skin care plan, ensure safety checks, and arrange immunology follow-up with HSCT discussion.

steps: ASK: symptoms, infection pattern, eczema/skin breakdown, meds, allergies, vaccines, family history
ORDER_LAB: CBC/diff, CMP (incl. LFTs/Cr), Ig panel incl. IgE; cultures if symptomatic
GET_RESULTS: assess for active infection and baseline organ function
DIAGNOSE: hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5 (AR) and implications
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm beta-lactam reaction and latex allergy
CHECK_INTERACTION: review interactions (esp. if considering azoles/macrolides)
PRESCRIBE: prophylactic antimicrobial plan consistent with allergy/contraindications; include monitoring orders
EDUCATE: diagnosis/inheritance/genetic counseling; treatment goals; skin care; urgent care triggers; side effects/monitoring; vaccines/lifestyle
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: immunology referral and follow-up timeline; discuss HSCT evaluation option
END: confirm understanding and return precautions

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 14-year-old girl. Speak vaguely and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless asked follow-up questions. Start with: “My joints keep swelling and hurting, and it’s getting harder to walk and use my hands.” Early on, volunteer: morning knee/ankle swelling and pain, long morning stiffness, wrist/finger pain affecting writing/opening bottles, and fatigue hurting school performance. If asked for details, add: swelling comes and goes but never fully resolves, hip/lower back deep ache after sitting, occasional low fever/flu-ish feeling with flares, episodes of red light-sensitive painful eyes, decreased appetite/weight loss, missed sports/PE. Keep hidden unless specifically asked: night pain waking you, limping, jaw soreness with chewing, knee can’t fully straighten, feet swelling/tight shoes, belly pain/loose stools during flares. Resist discussing mood, skipping meds, and avoiding friends unless the clinician is very supportive. Mention misconceptions: worry about Lyme from last summer tick bite, weather/growing pains, overdoing workouts, mouth sores/lupus. Minimize costs: act like specialists, labs, and PT are “optional,” and distrust generics. If shown empathy, soften slightly but stay guarded.

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (monogenic autoinflammatory arthritis) due to LACC1 deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: Monogenic juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 mutation
required_evidence: Chronic inflammatory arthritis beginning in childhood with persistent joint swelling/pain and morning stiffness
Elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and/or CRP) during active disease
Serologies often negative or low-titer (RF negative and anti-CCP negative) supporting a seronegative phenotype
Ferritin may be elevated as an acute-phase reactant (evaluate for systemic inflammation; screen for MAS if very high with cytopenias)
Known pathogenic LACC1 mutation or strong prior genetic confirmation consistent with monogenic disease

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (inflammatory leukocytosis possible during flares); evaluate for cytopenias if concern for macrophage activation syndrome
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Mild normocytic anemia of chronic inflammation may be present
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to moderately elevated as acute-phase reactant; markedly high would raise concern for systemic inflammation/MAS and warrants urgent evaluation

medications: Naproxen 10 mg/kg/dose PO twice daily with food (max 500 mg/dose) for symptom control if no contraindications
Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m^2 PO or SC once weekly (max 25 mg/week) as disease-modifying therapy
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg PO once weekly, not on methotrexate day) to reduce methotrexate toxicity
Adalimumab 24 mg/m^2 SC every 2 weeks (max 40 mg every 2 weeks) if inadequate response/intolerance to methotrexate or moderate-severe disease
Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day PO short course with rapid taper only for severe flare/bridging therapy while DMARD/biologic takes effect (avoid long-term use)
non_pharmacological: Pediatric rheumatology referral/ongoing co-management; confirm genetic diagnosis and phenotype if not already established
Physical and occupational therapy: range-of-motion, strengthening, joint protection; individualized exercise plan
Screening for uveitis per JIA protocols (ophthalmology slit-lamp exam schedule based on risk category)
Vaccination review: avoid live vaccines while on biologics/high-dose immunosuppression; ensure inactivated vaccines up to date
Baseline and periodic monitoring for therapy toxicity (CBC, LFTs for methotrexate; infection screening prior to biologic per local protocol)
Pain management plan and school/activity accommodations as needed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks after initiating/adjusting DMARD (earlier in 1-2 weeks if severe flare or systemic features); then every 8-12 weeks once stable

must_include: Condition is a chronic inflammatory arthritis beginning in childhood; in this case linked to a LACC1 genetic defect
Goal is control of inflammation to prevent joint damage, preserve function, and maintain quality of life
Explain that RF/anti-CCP are often negative in this phenotype and do not rule out inflammatory arthritis
NSAID is for symptom relief; methotrexate/biologic are to control disease and prevent damage

Monitoring and adverse effects: methotrexate (GI upset, mouth sores, liver enzyme elevation, cytopenias); biologics (infection risk); steroids (weight gain, mood changes, hypertension, bone effects)
Infection precautions and when to seek care (fever, severe illness); hold biologic during serious infection as directed

Shared decision-making around escalation to biologic therapy and balancing benefits vs risks
Importance of adherence to weekly methotrexate dosing and folic acid; avoid taking methotrexate daily
Encourage regular low-impact activity and PT/OT exercises; joint protection strategies
Avoid alcohol (or strictly limit per clinician guidance) due to methotrexate hepatotoxicity risk; discuss contraception/teratogenicity if applicable by age
Plan for school/activity accommodations and psychosocial support

Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; specifically assess NSAID–lithium interaction risk and other interactions (e.g., methotrexate with TMP-SMX, hepatotoxins).

Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; ensure no contraindications to NSAIDs, methotrexate, steroids, or biologics (pregnancy, significant liver disease, severe renal impairment, active serious infection, untreated TB/hepatitis, severe cytopenias).

Arrange follow-up to assess response and toxicity (e.g., 4–8 weeks after MTX start/adjustment; sooner if severe symptoms), and set lab monitoring cadence; define escalation timeline to biologic if not controlled.
Close encounter after ensuring safety netting, follow-up plan, and patient/caregiver understanding.

Interpret supportive findings (e.g., MRI DWI cortical ribboning/basal ganglia, CSF RT-QuIC/14-3-3, EEG periodic sharp wave complexes) and exclude reversible etiologies.
State suspected/probable CJD based on clinical course plus supportive tests and exclusion of treatable mimics; communicate uncertainty pending confirmatory testing.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, prognosis, supportive treatment focus, home safety (driving cessation, supervision, fall risk), and discuss palliative care and advance care planning.
Required safety check before any prescribing for symptom control.
Optional (non-critical) interaction screening prior to symptomatic prescribing, especially for sedatives/antipsychotics/antiepileptics.
If symptoms warrant, prescribe supportive symptom-targeted medications (e.g., for myoclonus, agitation, anxiety) with monitoring plan for sedation, falls, QTc risk, and caregiver instructions.
Arrange urgent neurology follow-up, expedited diagnostics, caregiver support, and palliative care referral; define return precautions.
Close after ensuring understanding, safety plan, and next steps.

nl_assertions: Asks about rapidity of cognitive decline (weeks to months) and documents key associated features: myoclonus, ataxia/gait instability, visual disturbance, behavioral/psychiatric changes, sleep disturbance, and functional loss.

Screens for reversible mimics and red flags requiring alternate pathways: delirium/toxic-metabolic causes, CNS infection/encephalitis, autoimmune encephalitis, thyroid disease, B12 deficiency, neurosyphilis/HIV risk, medication/substance effects, stroke, mass lesion, normal pressure hydrocephalus.
Assesses immediate safety: driving/operating machinery, falls, wandering, access to weapons, ability to manage meds/finances, need for supervision, caregiver strain, and capacity for decisions.
Recommends urgent neurology-led evaluation and parallel testing rather than presenting CJD as definitively confirmed without supportive data.

Explains diagnosis milestone content: CJD is a rare prion disease causing rapidly progressive brain dysfunction; diagnosis relies on clinical course plus supportive tests (MRI/CSF/EEG) and ruling out treatable causes; prognosis is poor with rapid progression and focus often shifts to comfort and safety.

Explains treatment milestone content: no proven disease-modifying cure; care is supportive and symptom-focused; meds may be used for agitation/anxiety/psychosis/myoclonus with careful monitoring for sedation and falls; plan includes urgent confirmatory workup and evaluation for reversible mimics.

Addresses concerns milestone content: discusses expected trajectory and uncertainty until testing is complete; reviews home safety (supervision, driving cessation, fall risk); offers palliative care and psychosocial supports for patient/caregivers.
Educates lifestyle milestone content: no driving/operating machinery; implement home safety measures and reliable caregiver support; encourages advance care planning and documentation of wishes.
Does not recommend unproven ‘curative’ therapies (e.g., antivirals/antibiotics/steroids) as CJD treatment unless specifically treating a plausible alternative diagnosis under evaluation.

If prescribing any medication, completes critical checks: allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing; interaction check is performed when available or agent states rationale if omitted.
Uses cautious language, avoids deterministic statements, and demonstrates empathy while maintaining clinical clarity; checks understanding and invites questions.
Provides clear return precautions (e.g., acute worsening, inability to keep patient safe at home, severe agitation, falls, aspiration risk) and escalation plan.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 32-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are anxious but communicate clearly and directly. Start with: “I’ve been having on-and-off pain on the lower right side of my belly for about 6 weeks and it’s starting to worry me.” Early in the visit, volunteer: intermittent ache/cramp RLQ sometimes sharper for minutes, bloating after meals, nausea without vomiting, and “weird” bowel habits alternating normal with a couple days of loose stools.

If asked, add: not clearly tied to eating but can flare with activity or big meals; 1–2 day episodes of watery diarrhea; cramping with bowel movements relieved after going; occasional brief flushing of face/neck especially with alcohol/stress; mild appetite decrease and a few pounds unintentional weight loss; no fever/chills; no visible blood in stool.

Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about other symptoms: wheezing/chest tightness, heart racing during flushing, fatigue, or RLQ tenderness when pressing.

Be reluctant/embarrassed about urgency and occasional stool staining; disclose only with gentle, nonjudgmental questioning. Voice misconceptions: “I think it’s IBS,” food intolerance, lingering bug, or appendicitis worry. If the clinician shows empathy, soften and share more. Express strong cost/time-off-work concerns and preference to manage in primary care; ask about cheaper options.

Tumor size documented (key cutoff at 2 cm; note 1–2 cm as intermediate-risk group)
Margin status from appendectomy specimen (R0 vs positive margin)
Depth of invasion/mesoappendiceal invasion and presence/absence of lymphovascular invasion
Tumor location (base vs tip) and any cecal involvement
Nodal status if assessed (imaging or surgical pathology)

If tumor >2 cm OR positive margin OR cecal involvement OR clinically/radiographically suspicious nodes: recommend right hemicolectomy with lymph node evaluation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital factor XIII deficiency
required_evidence: Bleeding phenotype consistent with FXIII deficiency (e.g., delayed bleeding after trauma/surgery, umbilical stump bleeding in infancy, poor wound healing, recurrent soft-tissue/muscle bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage risk)
Normal platelet count and typically normal PT/INR and aPTT despite bleeding history (not provided in this lab set but should be recognized as typical)
CBC may show anemia if chronic/recurrent bleeding; iron studies may show iron deficiency if ongoing blood loss
Exclusion of common alternative causes of bleeding suggested by available labs (no thrombocytopenia; no other cytopenias suggesting marrow failure)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: May be low if recent/chronic bleeding; otherwise can be normal

expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Often low/low-normal if iron deficiency from chronic blood loss; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal (FXIII deficiency is not a platelet disorder)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: May be low if iron deficiency due to chronic blood loss; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Serum iron: May be low if iron deficiency is present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.TIBC: May be high in iron deficiency; otherwise normal

correct_diagnosis.primary: Trigeminal neuralgia
required_evidence: Recurrent, sudden, brief (seconds to 2 minutes) unilateral facial pain in trigeminal nerve distribution
Pain described as electric shock-like/shooting/stabbing with severe intensity
Triggered by innocuous stimuli (e.g., talking, chewing, brushing teeth, light touch) with trigger zones
Pain-free intervals between attacks
No prominent cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, rhinorrhea) that would suggest cluster headache
No sustained neurologic deficits on exam; if deficits present, consider secondary causes and urgent imaging

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal at baseline; must be checked prior to and during carbamazepine therapy to monitor for leukopenia/agranulocytosis
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal at baseline; monitor for rare aplastic anemia
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Typically normal at baseline; monitor for thrombocytopenia
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Sodium: May be normal at baseline; monitor for hyponatremia (SIADH risk) with carbamazepine

medications: Carbamazepine 100 mg PO twice daily, titrate by 100-200 mg/day every 3-7 days to effect (typical maintenance 400-800 mg/day in divided doses; max 1200 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Avoid known triggers when possible (chewing on affected side, cold wind, facial touch) while maintaining nutrition and oral hygiene
If inadequate control or intolerable adverse effects: refer to neurology and discuss procedural options (microvascular decompression, percutaneous rhizotomy, stereotactic radiosurgery)
Consider brain MRI with and without contrast if atypical features (sensory loss, bilateral symptoms, age <40, poor response) to evaluate secondary causes (e.g., tumor, MS)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks after initiation/titration; sooner if rash, fever, sore throat, bruising/bleeding, confusion, or severe dizziness occurs

must_include: Trigeminal neuralgia causes brief, severe, shock-like facial pain from irritation of the trigeminal nerve
Attacks can be triggered by light touch/chewing/talking and occur in bursts with pain-free intervals
Why cluster headache is less likely: lacks prominent autonomic symptoms (tearing, nasal congestion) and pain is not typically continuous 15-180 minute orbital/temporal attacks
Carbamazepine is first-line and is started low then titrated to the lowest effective dose
Common adverse effects: dizziness, drowsiness, nausea, unsteadiness

Serious adverse effects requiring urgent stop/medical attention: rash/blistering (SJS/TEN), fever/sore throat (agranulocytosis), unusual bruising/bleeding (cytopenias), confusion/severe fatigue (hyponatremia)
Review allergies (penicillin, shellfish) and clarify no known cross-reactivity with carbamazepine, but any new rash is urgent
Discuss pregnancy/contraception considerations if applicable: carbamazepine is teratogenic and induces hepatic enzymes reducing efficacy of hormonal contraception
Advise avoiding alcohol and caution with driving/operating machinery until medication effects are known
Identify and minimize triggers while maintaining oral hygiene and adequate nutrition
Keep a pain/trigger diary to guide titration and assess response
Provide clear return precautions and follow-up plan for labs and symptom reassessment

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, onset/course, functional impact, family history, and screen for red flags or alternative diagnoses.
Clarify current medications/substances (including alcohol/sedatives), prior spasticity treatments, comorbidities, pregnancy status if relevant, and driving/work safety concerns.

State working diagnosis of hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP) based on history and typical progressive lower-extremity spasticity pattern; acknowledge differential and need for targeted testing if atypical.
Explain diagnosis in patient-friendly terms including typical course, variability, and role of clinical assessment/genetics; address mimic conditions and when further testing is needed.
Perform critical safety check before prescribing and confirm/clarify documented penicillin allergy and reaction details.
Perform critical drug-drug/substance interaction check before prescribing antispasticity medication (including additive sedation with alcohol/other sedatives).

Contraindication screen prior to prescribing (renal/hepatic disease, baseline sedation/falls, hypotension, pregnancy/breastfeeding, occupational driving/machinery), and assess spasticity severity/goals.
Initiate symptom-management therapy (e.g., baclofen) using start-low/go-slow approach with counseling on side effects and avoiding abrupt discontinuation.

Explain treatment goals, baclofen administration and adverse effects, safety precautions (falls, driving), and escalation options (tizanidine, botulinum toxin, intrathecal baclofen) if inadequate response.
Lifestyle and rehab counseling: daily stretching, low-impact exercise, adherence to PT, fall-prevention strategies, and avoidance of alcohol/sedatives during titration.
Arrange follow-up to assess response/tolerability, titrate medication, and reassess for red flags; consider referral to PT/neurology as appropriate.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and plan.
